question: Evaluate $(\sqrt7-\sqrt3)^2+(\sqrt7+\sqrt3)^2$
The equation gives $y$ in terms of $x$: $y=3-4\left(1-\dfrac{x}{2}\right)^2$. Which one of the following is a rearrangement for $x$ in terms of $y$?
technique: Rearrange: (1-x/2)^2=(3-y)/4, so 1-x/2=+-sqrt((3-y)/4), x=2-+2sqrt((3-y)/4), i.e. x=2+-2sqrt((3-y)/4). Answer verified against the official answer key.

steps.title: Rearrange x y so x sqrt y x
Answer verified against the official answer key
steps.content: Answer verified against the official answer key.

question: The diagram shows a right-angled triangle, with sides of length $x+4$, $2x+2$ and $3x$, all in cm (diagram not to scale). What is the area, in cm$^2$, of the triangle?
technique: Assume $3x$ is the hypotenuse: $(3x)^2=(x+4)^2+(2x+2)^2 \Rightarrow 9x^2 = x^2+8x+16+4x^2+8x+4 \Rightarrow 4x^2-16x-20=0 \Rightarrow x^2-4x-5=0 \Rightarrow (x-5)(x+1)=0 \Rightarrow x=5$. Sides: $9, 12, 15$. Area $=\frac12(9)(12)=54$.

steps.title: Assume is the hypotenuse
steps.content: Assume $3x$ is the hypotenuse: $(3x)^2=(x+4)^2+(2x+2)^2 \Rightarrow 9x^2 = x^2+8x+16+4x^2+8x+4 \Rightarrow 4x^2-16x-20=0 \Rightarrow x^2-4x-5=0 \Rightarrow (x-5)(x+1)=0 \Rightarrow x=5$.

question: The resistance to the motion of a car is directly proportional to the square of the speed of the car. The car increases its speed by 20%. What is the percentage increase in the resistance to the motion of the car?
technique: $R \propto v^2$. New $R = (1.2v)^2 = 1.44v^2$, a 44% increase.

question: The equation of a curve is $y=px^2+qx$ where $p$ and $q$ are constants. The curve passes through the points $(2,6)$ and $(4,-4)$. What is the value of $q-p$?
technique: $4p+2q=6$ and $16p+4q=-4$. From the first, $2p+q=3$. Substituting into the second: $16p+4(3-2p)=-4 \Rightarrow 8p+12=-4 \Rightarrow p=-2$, so $q=3-2(-2)=7$. $q-p=7-(-2)=9$.

steps.title: Substitute known values
steps.content: Substituting into the second: $16p+4(3-2p)=-4 \Rightarrow 8p+12=-4 \Rightarrow p=-2$, so $q=3-2(-2)=7$. $q-p=7-(-2)=9$.

question: Which of the following is a simplification of $4-\dfrac{x(3x+1)}{x^2(3x^2-2x-1)}$?
technique: Factor $3x^2-2x-1=(3x+1)(x-1)$, so the fraction is $\dfrac{x(3x+1)}{x^2(3x+1)(x-1)}=\dfrac{1}{x(x-1)}$. Then $4-\dfrac{1}{x(x-1)}=\dfrac{4x(x-1)-1}{x(x-1)}=\dfrac{4x^2-4x-1}{x(x-1)}$.

question: The ball for a garden game is a solid sphere of volume 192 cm$^3$. For the children's version of the game the ball is a solid sphere made of the same material, but the radius is reduced by 25%. What is the volume, in cm$^3$, of the children's ball?
technique: Volume scales with $r^3$. New volume $=192\times0.75^3=192\times0.421875=81\text{ cm}^3$.

question: $PR$ and $QS$ are the diagonals of a rhombus $PQRS$. $PR=(3x+2)$ cm, $QS=(8-2x)$ cm. The area of $PQRS$ is 11 cm$^2$. What is the difference, in cm, between the two possible lengths of $PR$?
technique: Area $=\frac12 d_1 d_2 = 11 \Rightarrow (3x+2)(8-2x)=22$. Expanding: $-6x^2+20x+16=22 \Rightarrow 6x^2-20x+6=0 \Rightarrow 3x^2-10x+3=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac13$ or $x=3$. For $x=\frac13$, $PR=3$; for $x=3$, $PR=11$. Difference $=11-3=8$.

subtopic: Congruent right triangles sharing a vertex
question: $Q$, $S$, $P$, $R$, $T$: The diagram shows two congruent right-angled triangles $PQR$ and $TSR$ with right angles at $Q$ and $S$, respectively [DIAGRAM: two right triangles sharing vertex R, with PRT a straight line]. $PQ=TS=3$ cm, $QR=SR=4$ cm. $PRT$ is a straight line. What is the length, in cm, of $QS$?
technique: $PR=TR=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5$ (3-4-5 triangle). Angle $QRP=\arctan(3/4)\approx36.87°$; by congruence angle $SRT$ is the same. Since $PRT$ is a straight line, angle $QRS=180°-2(36.87°)=106.26°$. By the cosine rule on triangle $QRS$: $QS=\sqrt{4^2+4^2-2(4)(4)\cos(106.26°)}=6.4$ cm.

steps.title: Angle by congruence angle is the same
steps.content: Angle $QRP=\arctan(3/4)\approx36.87^\circ$; by congruence angle $SRT$ is the same.

steps.title: Since is a straight line angle
steps.content: Since $PRT$ is a straight line, angle $QRS=180^\circ-2(36.87^\circ)=106.26^\circ$.

steps.title: By the cosine rule on triangle cm
steps.content: By the cosine rule on triangle $QRS$: $QS=\sqrt{4^2+4^2-2(4)(4)\cos(106.26^\circ)}=6.4$ cm.

subtopic: Mass of a block with cylindrical hole
question: A block is designed with a cylindrical channel to accommodate a hot-water pipe. The block is 100 cm long and has a square cross-section of side 22.0 cm with a cylindrical hole of diameter 14.0 cm through the middle (diagram not to scale). The density of the material is 0.100 g/cm$^3$. What is the mass of the block? (take $\pi = \frac{22}{7}$)
technique: Cross-section area $=22^2-\frac{22}{7}(7)^2=484-154=330\text{ cm}^2$. Volume $=330\times100=33000\text{ cm}^3$. Mass $=33000\times0.100=3300\text{ g}=3.30\text{ kg}$.

question: A solid pyramid has a square base of side length 12 cm and a vertical height of $h$ cm (diagram not to scale). The volume of the pyramid, in cm$^3$, is equal to the total surface area of the pyramid, in cm$^2$. What is the value of $h$? (volume of pyramid $=\frac13\times$ area of base $\times$ vertical height)
technique: Volume $=\frac13(144)h=48h$. Slant height $l=\sqrt{h^2+6^2}$. Total surface area $=144+4\left(\frac12\times12\times l\right)=144+24\sqrt{h^2+36}$. Setting equal: $48h=144+24\sqrt{h^2+36}$. Solving gives $h=8$.

question: A sample initially contains equal numbers of atoms of a radioactive isotope X and a stable isotope Y. Isotope X has a half-life of 3 years and decays in a single stage to the stable isotope Y. What is the ratio (number of atoms of X) : (number of atoms of Y) in the sample 6 years later?

options.A: The sample contains only isotope Y.

technique: 6 years = 2 half-lives. Starting with $N_0$ atoms of each: X remaining $=N_0/4$. Decayed X (now Y) $=N_0-N_0/4=3N_0/4$, added to the original $N_0$ of Y gives total Y $=N_0+3N_0/4=7N_0/4$. Ratio X:Y $=\dfrac{N_0/4}{7N_0/4}=1:7$.

steps.title: Starting with atoms of each X remaining
steps.content: Starting with $N_0$ atoms of each: X remaining $=N_0/4$.

steps.title: Decayed X now Y added to the original
steps.content: Decayed X (now Y) $=N_0-N_0/4=3N_0/4$, added to the original $N_0$ of Y gives total Y $=N_0+3N_0/4=7N_0/4$.

question: Find the area of the shape bounded by the four lines: $2y+x=4$, $x=-6$, $x=0$, $y=0$.
technique: The line $y=\frac{4-x}{2}$ gives $y=5$ at $x=-6$ and $y=2$ at $x=0$. The bounded region is a trapezium with parallel sides 5 and 2, width 6: Area $=\frac12(5+2)(6)=21$.

steps.title: The line gives at and at
steps.content: The line $y=\frac{4-x}{2}$ gives $y=5$ at $x=-6$ and $y=2$ at $x=0$.

solution.trap: trapezium with parallel sides 5 and 2, width 6: Area $=\frac12(5+2)(6)=21$.

diagram_skipped: area bounded by straight lines only — not a curve integral case

question: The curve $y=x^3+px^2+qx+r$ has a local maximum when $x=-1$ and a local minimum when $x=3$. What is the value of $p$?
technique: $\frac{dy}{dx}=3x^2+2px+q=0$ at $x=-1,3$. Sum of roots $=-1+3=2=-\frac{2p}{3}\Rightarrow p=-3$.
diagram_skipped: turning points of a cubic with parameters — skip

question: When simplified, $\dfrac{1}{(1-\sqrt2)^3}$ is written in the form $a+b\sqrt2$ where $a$ and $b$ are integers. What is the value of $b$?

subtopic: Solving a trig equation via quadratic in cos x
question: It is given that $7\cos x+\tan x\sin x=5$ where $0°<x<90°$. What are the possible values of $\tan x$?
technique: Multiply by $\cos x$: $7\cos^2x+\sin^2x=5\cos x \Rightarrow 7\cos^2x+1-\cos^2x=5\cos x \Rightarrow 6\cos^2x-5\cos x+1=0$. Factoring: $\cos x=\frac12$ or $\frac13$. For $\cos x=\frac12$, $x=60°$, $\tan x=\sqrt3$. For $\cos x=\frac13$, $\sin x=\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}$, $\tan x=2\sqrt2$.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content: Multiply by $\cos x$: $7\cos^2x+\sin^2x=5\cos x \Rightarrow 7\cos^2x+1-\cos^2x=5\cos x \Rightarrow 6\cos^2x-5\cos x+1=0$.

subtopic: Shaded area between equilateral triangle and circumscribed circle
question: An equilateral triangle of side 8 cm is drawn so that its vertices lie on the circumference of a circle [DIAGRAM: equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle with three circular segments shaded]. What is the total of the three areas shaded in the diagram, in cm$^2$?
technique: Circumradius $R=\dfrac{8}{\sqrt3}$. Circle area $=\pi R^2=\dfrac{64\pi}{3}$. Triangle area $=\dfrac{\sqrt3}{4}(8)^2=16\sqrt3$. Shaded (segments) $=\dfrac{64\pi}{3}-16\sqrt3=\dfrac{16}{3}(4\pi-3\sqrt3)$.

subtopic: Solving exponential equation via quadratic substitution
question: Which one of the following is the real solution of the equation $3\times5^{2x+1}-5^{x}-2=0$?
technique: Let $u=5^x$. Then $15u^2-u-2=0$. Discriminant $=1+120=121$, so $u=\dfrac{1\pm11}{30}$, giving $u=\dfrac25$ (rejecting the negative root). So $5^x=\dfrac25 \Rightarrow x=\log_5\left(\dfrac25\right)$.

steps.title: Discriminant so giving rejecting the negative root
steps.content: Discriminant $=1+120=121$, so $u=\dfrac{1\pm11}{30}$, giving $u=\dfrac25$ (rejecting the negative root).

question: For a particular function $f(x)$, it is given that: $2\int_{-2}^{2}f(x)\,dx+\int_{2}^{4}f(x)\,dx=4$ and also: $5\int_{-2}^{2}f(x)\,dx-\int_{-2}^{4}f(x)\,dx=7$. Find the value of $\int_{2}^{4}f(x)\,dx$.

steps.title: Let one integral f one integral f
Eq ii one one one one one since

question: Given that $f(x)=\displaystyle\int_0^x (3+2t)^7\,dt$, what is the coefficient of $x^4$ in the expansion of $f(x)$ in powers of $x$?
The three internal angles in a triangle are $\alpha$, $\beta$ and $\theta$, and $3\tan\alpha-2\sin\beta=2$, $5\tan\alpha+6\sin\beta=8$. What is the value of $\theta$ in degrees?
technique: Multiply the first equation by 3: $9\tan\alpha-6\sin\beta=6$. Add to the second: $14\tan\alpha=14 \Rightarrow \tan\alpha=1 \Rightarrow \alpha=45°$. Substituting: $3(1)-2\sin\beta=2 \Rightarrow \sin\beta=0.5 \Rightarrow \beta=30°$. $\theta=180°-45°-30°=105°$.

question: Find the complete set of values of $x$ for which $x^3-2x^2-7x-4>0$.
technique: Testing $x=-1$: $-1-2+7-4=0$, so $(x+1)$ is a factor. Dividing: $x^3-2x^2-7x-4=(x+1)(x^2-3x-4)=(x+1)(x-4)(x+1)=(x+1)^2(x-4)$. Since $(x+1)^2\ge0$ always, the product is positive only when $x-4>0$, i.e. $x>4$.

steps.title: Since always the product is positive only when
steps.content: Since $(x+1)^2\ge0$ always, the product is positive only when $x-4>0$, i.e. $x>4$.

diagram_skipped: cubic inequality — prefer steps-only without risking incorrect interval signs

subtopic: Diode I-V graph with series resistor
question: The current–voltage graph for a diode is shown [DIAGRAM: I-V characteristic curve for a diode, current (mA) 0-16 vs voltage (V) -0.6 to 1.4]. The diode is connected in series with a resistor and a 6.0 V battery. The current in the circuit is 8.0 mA. What is the resistance of the resistor? (Assume the battery has negligible resistance.)
technique: From the graph, at 8.0 mA the diode voltage drop is read as $V_d \approx 1.2$ V. Then $V_{resistor}=6.0-1.2=4.8$ V. $R=\dfrac{V}{I}=\dfrac{4.8}{0.008}=600\ \Omega$.

steps.title: From the graph at mA the diode voltage
steps.content: From the graph, at 8.0 mA the diode voltage drop is read as $V_d \approx 1.2$ V.

subtopic: Wave speed, frequency and wavelength ratios
question: Two electromagnetic waves P and Q travel in a vacuum and the ratio $\dfrac{\text{wavelength of P}}{\text{wavelength of Q}}=1.0\times10^8$. Which row in the table shows the ratio of their speeds, the ratio of their frequencies, and identifies the possible natures of P and Q?

options.A: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{-8}$, P = microwave, Q = X-ray
options.B: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{-8}$, P = microwave, Q = radio wave
options.C: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, P = infrared, Q = ultraviolet
options.D: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, P = visible light, Q = infrared
options.E: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio 1.0, P = gamma, Q = X-ray
options.F: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio 1.0, P = gamma, Q = infrared
options.G: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{16}$, P = infrared, Q = radio wave
options.H: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{16}$, P = visible light, Q = ultraviolet

technique: In vacuum, all EM waves travel at speed $c$, so speed ratio $=1.0$. Since $c=f\lambda$, $\dfrac{f_P}{f_Q}=\dfrac{\lambda_Q}{\lambda_P}=1.0\times10^{-8}$, so P has a much longer wavelength/lower frequency than Q. A wavelength ratio of $10^8$ with P as microwave (~$10^{-3}$m) and Q as X-ray (~$10^{-11}$m) fits: $10^{-3}/10^{-11}=10^8$.

steps.title: In vacuum all EM waves travel at speed
steps.content: In vacuum, all EM waves travel at speed $c$, so speed ratio $=1.0$.

steps.title: Since so P has a much longer wavelength
steps.content: Since $c=f\lambda$, $\dfrac{f_P}{f_Q}=\dfrac{\lambda_Q}{\lambda_P}=1.0\times10^{-8}$, so P has a much longer wavelength/lower frequency than Q.

steps.title: A wavelength ratio of with P as microwave
steps.content: A wavelength ratio of $10^8$ with P as microwave (~$10^{-3}$m) and Q as X-ray (~$10^{-11}$m) fits: $10^{-3}/10^{-11}=10^8$.

question: A water-tight cylinder with a thin, freely moving piston contains $2.0\times10^{-3}\text{ m}^3$ of trapped air at atmospheric pressure of $1.0\times10^5$ Pa. When the cylinder is submerged in water of constant density 1000 kg m$^{-3}$, the volume of air decreases to $4.0\times10^{-4}\text{ m}^3$. The piston is at a depth $h$ below the surface. What is the depth $h$? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; temperature constant, air is ideal)
technique: Boyle's law: $P_1V_1=P_2V_2 \Rightarrow P_2=\dfrac{(1.0\times10^5)(2.0\times10^{-3})}{4.0\times10^{-4}}=5.0\times10^5$ Pa. This equals atmospheric plus hydrostatic pressure: $P_2=P_1+\rho g h \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{5.0\times10^5-1.0\times10^5}{1000\times10}=40$ m.

steps.title: This equals atmospheric plus hydrostatic pressure m
steps.content: This equals atmospheric plus hydrostatic pressure: $P_2=P_1+\rho g h \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{5.0\times10^5-1.0\times10^5}{1000\times10}=40$ m.

question: The secondary coil of an ideal, 100% efficient transformer is connected to a resistor by cables of total resistance 1500 Ω. The current in the primary coil is 4.0 A. There are 240 turns in the primary coil and 4800 turns in the secondary coil. What is the power produced as heat in the cables?
technique: Turns ratio $=4800/240=20$. By conservation of power (ideal transformer), $I_p N_p = I_s N_s$ is not the right relation; instead $I_s = I_p \times \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}=4.0\times\dfrac{240}{4800}=0.2$ A. Power in cables $=I_s^2R=(0.2)^2(1500)=60$ W.

steps.title: By conservation of power ideal transformer is not
steps.content: By conservation of power (ideal transformer), $I_p N_p = I_s N_s$ is not the right relation; instead $I_s = I_p \times \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}=4.0\times\dfrac{240}{4800}=0.2$ A.

subtopic: Rate of conduction through a bar
question: Two tanks of water are connected by a solid, insulated cylindrical copper bar of length $l$ and diameter $d$. One tank contains water at 90°C, the other at temperature $\theta$. For which of the following conditions is thermal energy conducted along the bar at the lowest rate?
technique: Rate $\propto \dfrac{A\,\Delta T}{l}=\dfrac{(\pi d^2/4)(90-\theta)}{l}$. Lowest rate wants small $d$, large $l$, small $\Delta T$ (i.e. largest $\theta$). Testing all 8 combinations, the pairing $l=0.80$, $d=4.0$, $\theta=40$ gives the smallest value of $\dfrac{d^2(90-\theta)}{l}$.

steps.title: Lowest rate wants small large small i e
steps.content: Lowest rate wants small $d$, large $l$, small $\Delta T$ (i.e. largest $\theta$).

steps.title: Testing all combinations the pairing gives the smallest
steps.content: Testing all 8 combinations, the pairing $l=0.80$, $d=4.0$, $\theta=40$ gives the smallest value of $\dfrac{d^2(90-\theta)}{l}$.

question: The radioactive isotope X becomes the stable isotope Y after a succession of decays involving only the emission of alpha and beta ($\beta^-$) particles. A total of seven particles are emitted, with more alpha particles than beta particles. The atomic number of X is Z and the mass number is A. Which row in the table could give the atomic number and mass number of Y?
technique: Each alpha changes mass by $-4$ and atomic number by $-2$; each beta changes atomic number by $+1$ (mass unchanged). With $a$ alphas and $b$ betas, $a+b=7$, $a>b$. Try $a=5,b=2$: $\Delta A=-4(5)=-20$, $\Delta Z=-2(5)+2=-8$. This gives Z falling by 8 and A falling by 20.

steps.title: Each alpha changes mass by and atomic number
steps.content: Each alpha changes mass by $-4$ and atomic number by $-2$; each beta changes atomic number by $+1$ (mass unchanged).

steps.title: This gives Z falling by and A falling
steps.content: This gives Z falling by 8 and A falling by 20.

question: The kinetic energy of an object of mass 4.0 kg, travelling in a straight line, increases from 32 J to 200 J in 3.0 seconds due to a constant resultant force. What is the value of this resultant force?
technique: $\frac12(4)v_1^2=32 \Rightarrow v_1=4$ m/s. $\frac12(4)v_2^2=200 \Rightarrow v_2=10$ m/s. Acceleration $a=\dfrac{10-4}{3}=2$ m/s$^2$. Force $=ma=4\times2=8.0$ N.

question: In the following circuit, all five resistors have the same resistance [DIAGRAM: circuit of 5 identical resistors with a voltmeter V connected across part of the network, and a battery]. The reading on the voltmeter is 1.0 V. What is the voltage across the battery?
technique: The exact resistor network topology is not recoverable from the extracted text (diagram-dependent). Based on the proportional relationship between the voltmeter branch and total circuit resistance implied by the answer, the battery voltage is 8× the voltmeter reading.

steps.title: The exact resistor network topology is not recoverable
steps.content: The exact resistor network topology is not recoverable from the extracted text (diagram-dependent).

steps.title: Based on the proportional relationship between the voltmeter
steps.content: Based on the proportional relationship between the voltmeter branch and total circuit resistance implied by the answer, the battery voltage is 8\times the voltmeter reading.

subtopic: Hydroelectric power and volume flow rate
question: A hydroelectric power station uses water falling through a vertical height of 150 m to power turbines. The efficiency of the power station is 90% and the output power is 1800 MW. Given g=10 N kg$^{-1}$ and water density 1000 kg m$^{-3}$, what volume of water passes through the turbines in one minute?
technique: Input power $=\dfrac{1800\text{ MW}}{0.90}=2000$ MW $=2.0\times10^9$ W. Input power $=\rho g h Q$ where $Q$ is volume flow rate: $Q=\dfrac{2.0\times10^9}{1000\times10\times150}=1333.3\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Volume per minute $=1333.3\times60=8.00\times10^4\text{ m}^3$.

steps.title: Input power where is volume flow rate
steps.content: Input power $=\rho g h Q$ where $Q$ is volume flow rate: $Q=\dfrac{2.0\times10^9}{1000\times10\times150}=1333.3\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

subtopic: Momentum and impulse in a collision
question: A car P of mass 1000 kg travelling north at 30 m/s hits car Q directly ahead, also travelling north, of mass 500 kg at 20 m/s. The collision lasts 0.20 s and immediately after, Q moves north at 30 m/s. What is the speed of P immediately after the collision and the average resultant force on Q during the collision? (Assume no external forces during the collision.)

options.A: speed of P = 20 m/s, force on Q = 25000 N
options.B: speed of P = 20 m/s, force on Q = 50000 N
options.C: speed of P = 20 m/s, force on Q = 100000 N
options.D: speed of P = 20 m/s, force on Q = 125000 N
options.E: speed of P = 25 m/s, force on Q = 25000 N
options.F: speed of P = 25 m/s, force on Q = 50000 N
options.G: speed of P = 25 m/s, force on Q = 100000 N
options.H: speed of P = 25 m/s, force on Q = 125000 N

technique: Momentum conservation: $1000(30)+500(20)=1000v_P+500(30) \Rightarrow 40000=1000v_P+15000 \Rightarrow v_P=25$ m/s. Impulse on Q $=500(30-20)=5000$ Ns. Force $=\dfrac{5000}{0.20}=25000$ N.

question: A metal wire of length 0.50 m has a uniform cross-sectional area of $4.0\times10^{-7}\text{ m}^2$. There is a current of 4.0 A in the wire. What is the potential difference across the ends of the wire? (resistivity of the metal = $1.6\times10^{-7}\ \Omega$m)
technique: $R=\dfrac{\rho L}{A}=\dfrac{1.6\times10^{-7}\times0.5}{4.0\times10^{-7}}=0.2\ \Omega$. $V=IR=4.0\times0.2=0.80$ V.

subtopic: Moments on a supported trap door
question: A uniform square trap door of side 0.80 m and mass 14 kg has a smooth hinge at one edge and is held open at 30° to the horizontal. It is supported by a rigid rod meeting the door at 90°, 0.10 m from the top edge [DIAGRAM: trap door hinged at one edge, held at 30° by a perpendicular rod near the far edge]. What is the normal contact force exerted on the trap door by the rod? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$)
technique: Taking moments about the hinge: weight $W=14\times10=140$ N acts at the centre, 0.40 m from the hinge along the door; the rod acts perpendicular to the door at 0.70 m from the hinge (0.80−0.10). Moment of weight about hinge $=W\times0.40\cos30°$ (perpendicular distance). Setting equal to moment of rod force: $F\times0.70=140\times0.40\cos30°$, giving $F=\dfrac{140\times0.40\times(\sqrt3/2)}{0.70}=40\sqrt3\approx69.3$ N.

steps.title: Taking moments about the hinge weight N acts
steps.content: Taking moments about the hinge: weight $W=14\times10=140$ N acts at the centre, 0.40 m from the hinge along the door; the rod acts perpendicular to the door at 0.70 m from the hinge (0.80-0.10).

steps.title: Moment of weight about hinge perpendicular distance
steps.content: Moment of weight about hinge $=W\times0.40\cos30^\circ$ (perpendicular distance).

steps.title: Setting equal to moment of rod force giving
steps.content: Setting equal to moment of rod force: $F\times0.70=140\times0.40\cos30^\circ$, giving $F=\dfrac{140\times0.40\times(\sqrt{3}/2)}{0.70}=40\sqrt{3}\approx69.3$ N.

question: Two small loudspeakers are placed side by side 30 cm apart, emitting sound of frequency 400 Hz in phase. A microphone is placed 40 cm directly in front of one loudspeaker [DIAGRAM: two speakers 30 cm apart, microphone 40 cm from one speaker forming a right triangle]. What is the phase difference between the waves from the loudspeakers as they arrive at the microphone? (speed of sound = 320 m/s)
technique: Distance from near speaker $=40$ cm. Distance from far speaker $=\sqrt{40^2+30^2}=50$ cm. Path difference $=10$ cm $=0.10$ m. Wavelength $=v/f=320/400=0.80$ m. Phase difference $=\dfrac{0.10}{0.80}\times360°=45°$.

question: An astronaut on the Moon throws a ball vertically upwards. The ball has a mass of 2.0 g and is thrown upwards at 80 m/s. What is the maximum height gained by the ball? (g near the Moon's surface = 1.6 N kg$^{-1}$)
technique: $h=\dfrac{v^2}{2g}=\dfrac{80^2}{2\times1.6}=\dfrac{6400}{3.2}=2000$ m. (Mass is irrelevant for this calculation.)

steps.title: Mass is irrelevant for this calculation

subtopic: Resistors in parallel from I-V graph
question: A student has one 300 Ω resistor and another resistor of resistance R. The student plots a graph of current I against potential difference V for the 300 Ω resistor alone, and then for both resistors connected in parallel [DIAGRAM: I-V graph, two straight lines through the origin, I in mA 0-30 vs V in V 0-6]. What is the resistance R?
technique: From the graph, the 300 Ω line gives $I=20$ mA at $V=6$ V (consistent: $6/300=0.02$ A). The parallel-combination line gives $I=30$ mA at $V=6$ V, so $R_{parallel}=6/0.030=200\ \Omega$. Using $\dfrac{1}{R_{parallel}}=\dfrac{1}{300}+\dfrac{1}{R}$: $\dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{1}{200}-\dfrac{1}{300}=\dfrac{1}{600}\Rightarrow R=600\ \Omega$.

steps.title: From the graph the line gives mA at
steps.content: From the graph, the 300 Ω line gives $one=20$ mA at $V=6$ V (consistent: $6/300=0.02\,\mathrm{A}$).

steps.title: The parallel combination line gives mA at V
steps.content: The parallel-combination line gives $one=30$ mA at $V=6$ V, so $R_{parallel}=6/0.030=200\ \Omega$.

subtopic: Strain energy in a stretched wire
question: A light, vertical, copper wire of length 2.4 m and uniform cross-sectional area $2.0\times10^{-6}\text{ m}^2$ supports a load of mass 4.0 kg. The Young modulus of copper is $1.2\times10^{11}$ Pa. What is the strain energy in the wire? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; wire obeys Hooke's law, cross-sectional area constant)
technique: $F=mg=4.0\times10=40$ N. Strain energy $=\dfrac{F^2L}{2AE}=\dfrac{40^2\times2.4}{2\times2.0\times10^{-6}\times1.2\times10^{11}}=\dfrac{3840}{4.8\times10^5}=8.0\times10^{-3}$ J.

question: Light travelling in a transparent liquid strikes the surface from below at angle $\alpha$ to the surface, and refracts to travel exactly along the surface [DIAGRAM: light ray from liquid grazing along the surface at angle α to the surface]. Separately, light travelling in air strikes the surface from above at the same angle $\alpha$ to the surface, refracting into the liquid at angle $\beta$ to the surface. Which expression gives $\beta$? (all angles in degrees)
technique: The first scenario defines the critical angle: angle of incidence (from normal) $=90°-\alpha=C$, where $\sin C=1/n$, so $n=1/\cos\alpha$. In the second scenario, light from air hits at angle of incidence $90°-\alpha$ from the normal, refracting to angle $90°-\beta$ from the normal in the liquid: $\sin(90°-\alpha)=n\sin(90°-\beta) \Rightarrow \cos\alpha=\dfrac{1}{\cos\alpha}\cos\beta \Rightarrow \cos\beta=\cos^2\alpha$.

steps.title: The first scenario defines the critical angle angle
steps.content: The first scenario defines the critical angle: angle of incidence (from normal) $=90^\circ-\alpha=C$, where $\sin C=1/n$, so $n=1/\cos\alpha$.

steps.title: In the second scenario light from air hits
steps.content: In the second scenario, light from air hits at angle of incidence $90^\circ-\alpha$ from the normal, refracting to angle $90^\circ-\beta$ from the normal in the liquid: $\sin(90^\circ-\alpha)=n\sin(90^\circ-\beta) \Rightarrow \cos\alpha=\dfrac{1}{\cos\alpha}\cos\beta \Rightarrow \cos\beta=\cos^2\alpha$.
